figures.caption: Figure 2: Empirical loss of single change region estimation using CRISP, averaged over 100 Monte Carlo repetitions, plotted against the sample size (top panels) and signal strengths (bottom panels) on log-log scale, for different dimensions. Data generating mechanism described in Section 3.2.

Figure 5: Maps of temperature difference between consecutive decades using the ERA5 and ESA data. Observations at 2000 randomly sampled grid points (blue crosses) are used to compute abnormal regions of temperature difference using the CRISP methodology. The estimated abnormal regions are shown as red discs on the maps.

Figure 4: Empirical results for multiple change region estimation using CRISP and competitors from lidR averaged over 100 Monte Carlo repetitions when 𝑑 = 3. Upper panel: losses plotted against the sample size 𝑛 on log-log scale. Lower panel: Adjusted Rand Index plotted against 𝑛 on log-log scale.

Figure 1: (a) A scan region 𝐵 contains multiple true change regions; an inner disc 𝐴 can aggregate parts of several regions. (b) A small scan region 𝐵 captures only a fragment of a true change region 𝑅, yet can still yield a significant contrast.

Figure 7: Example illustration of the points 𝑄 specified in (31) and 𝑅, the angle 𝜃 and the line segment of length 𝑟 when 𝑑 = 3 with 𝐴𝛼,𝛽 and 𝐴𝛼′ ,𝛽 shown as red discs. Panel (a) shows the projection of the sphere viewed from above. Panel (b) shows the projection of the sphere viewed from aside.
Figure 6: Ozone levels in the southern hemisphere in year 2005, 2015 and 2025. Detected abnormal regions using the CRISP method are shown as blue discs.

Figure 3: Empirical results for multiple change-region estimation using CRISP, averaged over 100 Monte Carlo repetitions. The loss is plotted against the sample size (top panels) and signal strengths (middle panels) on log-log scale, and the average number of estimated regions (true value: four) is shown in the bottom panels, for different dimensions. The data-generating mechanism is described in Section 3.2.
